guest: Scott Belsky, founder of Behance, Chief Product Officer at Adobe, author, investor

business_ideas.idea: Behance: searchable, sortable portfolio discovery platform for creatives (inverted model from artist websites)
Next-gen marketplace model: sustainable freelancer platforms with new value distribution (blockchain-driven or alternative mechanics)
Superpowers-based OS: replace app store paradigm with contextual, location/time-aware interfaces (sixth sense interface)

predictions.text: Apps will become obsolete; OS will evolve to context-aware superpowers based on GPS, AI, notifications
Freelancer marketplaces need new economics beyond transaction-fee rent-seeking model

dynamics: Shaan deliberately avoids standard interview format (skips 'first 100 customers' questions). Conversation is philosophical, hopping between topics. Scott is reflective, analytical, articulate. Both are aligned; tone is collaborative, friendly. Energy is high but thoughtful (9pm Christmas Eve recording). Scott is visibly engaged despite packed day.
frameworks: First mile obsession: onboarding + splash pages + progressive disclosure + defaults determine product success (only part every customer experiences)
Psychology over features: products engage via insecurities/validation needs; VSCO example (hiding follower counts + likes)
Things that don't scale are the art: Airbnb photographers, custom experiences that distinguish brands
Weekly calendar audits: look back, ask what advanced your priorities? Reveals misalignment with stated values
Energy audits: mark activities green (energizing) or red (draining); reallocate towards green over time
Step-function conversations: Do people leave you thinking differently? Selects for founders/people worth investing in
Hedging paradox: Building multiple products/doing business school simultaneously dilutes focus; killing features increases core usage
learnings: Media coverage obsesses over beginning/end (95%); 99% of hard work is the invisible messy middle
At lows, you make fear-based decisions (emulate competitors, lose identity); at highs, you misattribute success to your decisions
Founder passion for solution > customer empathy for problem causes wasted years; must spend time with customers observing real lives
Shipping multiple features as risk hedging actually dilutes engagement; killing features led to higher core usage at Behance
Career paths emerge from following curiosity, not declaring career goals (became founder, executive, investor organically)
Small investments for education (Pinterest when you can't afford it) pays off more than risk-hedging portfolio approach
Peer networks matter more than individual deals; curated relationships >algorithms for learning
Disconnection/deep thinking is competitive advantage in always-connected world
Best investors understand present deeply and how it's shifting, not just distant futures

banger_quotes.quote: 95% of media coverage is 1% of time (beginning/end). 99% is the messy middle.
The science of business is scaling. The art is the things that don't scale.
Every customer is lazy, vain, and selfish in their first 15-30 seconds.
When you ship features and kill a few, core stuff gets used more.
526,000 listeners in 6 months on a podcast I started on a whim.

numbers_dropped: Behance: 5 years bootstrapped, 2 years VC-backed, 3 years integration post-Adobe acquisition
Pinterest investment: ~2010, early stage, 'good chunk of salary' when couldn't afford it
Uber investment: pre-Travis, pre-Ryan, small check, product advisor role
Behance: killed Tip Exchange (Digg-like feature) + Groups feature; core usage increased post-deletion
The Messy Middle: 680+ Evernote insights distilled into ~200 book insights
MFM podcast: 526,000 listeners (from Shaan's intro) in ~6 months
notable: Shaan intentionally breaks interview formula (says no standard questions); Scott is unusually candid about hedging behavior (MBA + multiple products = mistake). 'The Messy Middle' book is meta-commentary on media obsessing over start/end while ignoring 99% of real work. Investment stories (Pinterest/Uber as education when poor) show different mindset than traditional financial investing. Recorded Christmas Eve; Scott demonstrates '80% of success is just showing up' philosophy.
